coredata.dc:title: Ginkgo leaf-based synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for highly sensitive detection of salazosulfapyridine in mouse plasma
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Xiaohua
Qin, Dongmiao
Mo, Guichun
Feng, Jinsu
Yu, Chunhe
Mo, Weiming
Deng, Biyang
coredata.dc:description: A simple, economical hydrothermal strategy for synthesizing nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) was developed using Ginko leaves as a carbon source. These N-CQDs have strong blue fluorescence, excitation-relevant emissions, high monodispersity, good stability, good water solubility, and a 22.8% fluorescence quantum yield. They average 3 nm in size, and have maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 350 and 436 nm, respectively. They are used as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for the label-free sensitive detection of salazosulfapyridine (SASP) due to the strong quenching effect of SASP. When SASP concentration is 0.1–80 μmol/L, there is a good linear relationship with a detection limit of 40 nmol/L. This method was successfully applied to detect SASP in mouse plasma. The results show that the SASP recovery range was 96%–101%. RSDs ranged from 2.6% to 3.1%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Salazosulfapyridine
Carbon quantum dots
Ginkgo leaf
Fluorescence
Plasma

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of N-CQDs preparation and SASP detection.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CQDs (12 μg/mL). Inset shows the photographs of N-CQDs (1 μg/mL) under visible light (yellow) and UV light (blue); (B) Fluorescence excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of N-CQDs; (C) Emission spectra of N-CQDs (1 μg/mL) at different excitation wavelength (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
(A) Effect of different concentration of SASP on fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs (1 μg/mL); (B) Relationship between SASP concentration and quenching efficiency.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of 1 μg/mL N-CQDs (a), 30 μmol/L SASP (b), 1 μg/mL N-CQDs + 30 μmol/L SASP (c), theoretical curve: curve a + curve b (d)；(B) Fluorescence intensity-based Stern-Volmer plot of N-CQDs in the presence of SASP; (C) Fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs in the absence and presence of SASP.
(A) Fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs (1 μg/mL) in presence of 60 μmol/L of anti-inflammatory analgesics drugs; (B) Coexisting common ions and other molecules.

head.ce:title: Ginkgo leaf-based synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for highly sensitive detection of salazosulfapyridine in mouse plasma

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources (Ministry of Education of China), School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin 541004 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources (Ministry of Education of China)
School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, economical hydrothermal strategy for synthesizing nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) was developed using Ginko leaves as a carbon source. These N-CQDs have strong blue fluorescence, excitation-relevant emissions, high monodispersity, good stability, good water solubility, and a 22.8% fluorescence quantum yield. They average 3 nm in size, and have maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 350 and 436 nm, respectively. They are used as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for the label-free sensitive detection of salazosulfapyridine (SASP) due to the strong quenching effect of SASP. When SASP concentration is 0.1–80 μmol/L, there is a good linear relationship with a detection limit of 40 nmol/L. This method was successfully applied to detect SASP in mouse plasma. The results show that the SASP recovery range was 96%–101%. RSDs ranged from 2.6% to 3.1%.

ce:section.ce:para: Salazosulfapyridine (SASP) is mainly used to treat inflammatory bowel disease, Crohn’s disease, and ulcerative colitis. Its main adverse reactions are allergic reactions, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, anaemia, hemolytic anemia, hemoglobinuria, liver and kidney damage, goiters, hypothyroidism, and insanity. A simple and effective method for detecting SASP is urgently needed. Presently, SASP detection methods include immunochemical [1], electrochemical [2], chromatographic [3], and combined technologies [4]. These methods are usually used in low sensitivity, complex operation procedures and by more highly-trained technicians. Therefore, a simple and rapid method with high sensitivity detection of SASP would be quite essential.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a group of carbon nanoparticles smaller than 10 nm [5]. CQDs have the advantages of large specific surface area, green synthesis, high photo stability, easy functionalization, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and good water solubility [6]. Compared to traditional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, CQDs show some supra-molecular properties, making them widely-applicable in biological imaging, drug delivery, photodynamic therapy, photovoltaic devices, catalysis, and sensing [7]. CQDs synthesis methods can be broadly divided into two categories: top-down; and, bottom-up approaches. The top-down method is based on different carbon sources, including electrochemical oxidation, acid stripping and oxidation, electron beam lithotripsy, and microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis [8]. Bottom-up synthesis usually uses organic molecules, or polymers, as precursors and is dehydrated and then carbonized to form CQDs. Most of these methods involve complex processes, special equipment, and have low yields. CQDs have a great potential in various applications, but need improvement by adjusting core and surface microstructures. Doping is an effective way to regulate the intrinsic properties of quantum dots, including electronic properties, and surface and local chemical reactivity [9]. Introducing atomic impurities, such as nitrogen, phosphorus, or boron can change their electronic structure. Doping can greatly improve CQDs quantum yields [10]. Nanomaterials are presently attracting more and more attention, particularly nanomaterial synthesis using waste biomass as raw material. Ginkgo leaf, mango peel, pineapple leaf, and banana peel, by-products of agriculture and industry, are major soil waste. Using this waste biomass to produce carbon materials can reduce soil pollution and make full use of resources. Ginkgo leaf is one of the most common waste biomass. It has been used to produce carbon materials due to its high yield, low cost, biodegradability, and recyclability.

We propose a relatively simple hydrothermal synthesis strategy for obtaining high light-emitting CQDs using Ginkgo leaf as the carbon source. These synthesized CQDs have acceptable photoluminescence stability, high water solubility, uniform size, and strong blue emission. SASP has a quenching effect on the prepared N-CQDs, this method can be used as an effective SASP fluorescence-sensing platform. It has been successfully applied to analyze SASP in mouse plasma. The schematic diagram of the synthesis of N-CQDs and detection of SASP is shown in Fig. 1 .

Salazosulfapyridine, LVFX, aspirin, sulfonamide, amoxicillin and sinomenine were purchased from the National Institute for the Control of Pharmaceutical and Biological Products (Beijing, China). Human serum albumin was purchased from the Beijing Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Company (Beijing, China). l-cysteine, glucose and urea were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Double-distilled water (DDW) was used throughout.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was performed with a JEM-2010 microscope (JEOL, Japan). It was operated at 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were obtained with a D/max–2500 V/PC powder X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan). Fourier-transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were recorded using a PerkinElmer FTIR spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out using an ESCALAB 250 photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Fluorescence lifetime experiments were performed using a FL3-P-TCSPC, time-resolved, fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, Longjumeau, France). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were characterized using a Cary 60 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). All fluorescence spectra were obtained on a RF-5301 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

CQDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using Ginkgo leaves as the carbon source. Fresh Ginkgo leaves were washed and then dried in a drying chamber at 70 ℃ for 12 h. They were then ground to powder. Then 1 g of the Ginkgo leaf powder was dissolved in 25 mL water. The solution was ultrasonicated for 10 min and transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The reaction kettle was heated at 200 ℃ for 10 h, then cooled to room temperature. The obtained brown solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. The N-CQDs solution was filtered using a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove the larger particles. A dialysis bag of 1000 MWCO was used to purify the CQDs for 24 h. And then the CQDs were stored at 4 ℃ for further use.

N-CQDs fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) was measured using quinine sulfate as the reference (0.1 mol/L H2SO4 aqueous solution, FLQY for 0.54) using the following equation:
Φx = Φstd × (Ix/Istd) × (Astd / Ax) × (nx/nstd)2
where Φ, I, A, and n represent fluorescence quantum yield, fluorescence intensity, absorbance, and the solvent refractive index, respectively. The subscript “std” refers to the reference (quinine sulfate in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 aqueous solution) with “x” representing the unknown sample.

A 25 μL N-CQDs solution (1 μg/mL) was mixed with different concentrations of an SASP solution. And then the solution was diluted to a total volume of 500 μL using a 10 mmol/L phosphate buffer solution (PBS). The solution fluorescence signals were determined at a 350 nm excitation wavelength after 1 min at room temperature.

The mice were fasted one day before the experiment with freely available water. The mice were fed 5 mg SASP/kg weight on the next morning. The control group was fed the same amount of DDW. Two hours after drug administration, 1 mL blood sample was collected via an eye vein. Blood samples were placed in a centrifuge tube treated with heparin sodium and treated according to the previous report [11].

Blood samples were centrifuged at 3500 rpm for 10 min to obtain a plasma sample. A 200 μL plasma sample was decanted into a centrifuge tube, and then, 100 μL of 0.1 mol/L NaOH and 1 mL ethyl acetate were added into the tube. The sample was then whirlpool-oscillated for 5 min. The solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min. The top ethyl acetate was decanted into another tube. The bottom residue was extracted twice using ethyl acetate. The extract was evaporated to dry under a stream of dry nitrogen at 80 °C. The tube inner wall was rinsed with 200 μL of methanol to concentrate the sample. The solvent was then evaporated. The remaining dry residue was dissolved in 200 μL of water for SASP measurement.

The synthesized N-CQDs morphologies were characterized using TEM. They were nearly spherical and dispersed among particles (Fig. S1A). This indicates good water solubility. One hundred carbon dots were randomly selected to calculate CQDs sizes according to a particle-size distribution histogram. N-CQDs particle size distribution was in the 2–4 nm range with the average particle size being about 3 nm (Fig. S1B). A high resolution TEM (HRTEM) diagram (Fig. S1A) shows that the crystal surface constant is 0.21 nm and that the graphite (100) crystal surface constant was uniform [12]. An XRD characterization was done in order to obtain the N-CQDs crystal plane data. The results show a wide central peak at 23.4° (Fig. S1C), which indicates that the interlayer spacing is 0.38 nm, which is slightly larger than that of graphene (0.34 nm). This result is believed to be due to the introduction of oxygen and nitrogen functional group [13].

The N-CQDs FTIR spectrum appears in Fig. S1D. The peaks at 3385 cm−1 and 2935 cm−1 are the stretching vibrations of OH and CH, respectively [14]. The strong absorption peak at 1661 cm−1 suggests the presence of CO. The peak at 1419 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C–H. CO stretching vibration can be identified by the peak at 1144 cm−1 [15]. An IR analysis shows the presence of O-rich groups on the carbon frame surface. Abundant oxygen-containing groups endow N-CQDs with good water solubility [16].

N-CQDs morphology was characterized using XPS. The XPS spectrum shows three strong binding energy peaks at 285.1, 399.5, and 532.0 eV. These are attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. S2 A). This is composed of three elements C (70.84%), O (24.98%), and N (4.18%). The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. S2B) shows four peaks at 284.5, 285.1, 286.2, and 288.6 eV (Fig. S2B) which are assigned to the CC/CC, COH/CN, CO, and COOR groups, respectively [17]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 3C) shows three peaks at 399.5, 400.0, and 401.4 eV (Fig. S2C) which are assigned to C N C, N(C)3, and NH, respectively. The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 3D) shows three peaks at 531.1, 532.0, and 532.9 eV (Fig. S2D), which are assigned to COH, CO, and COC, respectively. [18].

The N-CQDs UV–vis spectrum appears in Fig. 2 A. There is a weak absorption peak at 230 nm, attributable to π→π* transitions for the aromatic CC sp2 domains. The absorption peak at 280 nm is caused by the n→π* electron transition of CO [19]. The N-CQDs solution is yellow in natural light and bright blue in ultraviolet (Fig. 2A). This suggests strong N-CQDs fluorescence properties. N-CQDs strong fluorescence may be due to nitrogen-doped surface defects. The fluorescence spectra show that N-CQDs optimal excitation and emission wavelengths are 350 nm and 436 nm, respectively (Fig. 2B). Emission peak intensities increased when excitation wavelength increased from 300 nm to 350 nm, and then decreased as excitation wavelength increased. The emission peak red shift was observed (Fig. 2C). This may be due to N-CQDs surface defects and particle size differences or N-CQDs surface luminescence site differences. Using quinine sulfate as a reference, N-CQDs quantum yield (QY) was calculated to be 22.8% using the process described in Section 2.4.

The effects of pH value, ion strength, storage times, and irradiation times of 365 nm ultraviolet lamp on synthesized N-CQDs stability were studied to determine whether they have practical applications. N-CQDs fluorescence intensity increased in the pH range from 1 to 6 and then weakened as the pH values varied from 6 to 12 (Fig. S3A). Fluorescence intensity reached a maximum when pH was 6. This may be due to functional groups, such as NH and OH, on the N-CQDs surfaces which may form intramolecular and intermolecular hydrogen bonds. This endows N-CQDs with different surface states. As pH varies, the deprotonation or protonation of the functional groups can lead to surface state and fluorescence variations. Except for excitation and pH tunable fluorescence, solvent polarity can also tune the CQDs fluorescence emissions [20]. N-CQDs fluorescence intensities did not change markedly when NaCl concentrations changed from 0 to 1 mol/L. This suggests that N-CQDs have satisfactory salt resistance. N-CQDs fluorescence intensity did not change significantly after 2 h of exposure to 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. This suggests an adequate anti-ultraviolet radiation quality (Fig. S3C). N-CQDs fluorescence after being stored for 2.5 weeks showed no obvious changes. This indicates that N-CQDs are stable and convenient for long-term storage (Fig. S3D).

pH and reaction times were examined in order to determine the optimal conditions for SASP detection. The SASP quenching efficiency of N-CQDs first increased and then decreased as solution pH value varied from 4 to 9. Quenching efficiency reached its maximum at pH 6 (Fig. S4 A). Therefore, this experiment used pH 6. The reaction time was 1 min when the quenching efficiency was at its highest (Fig. S4B). The detection time used in this experiment was 1 min.

The results are shown in Fig. 3 A. N-CQDs fluorescence intensities increased as SASP concentrations increased. The relationship between quenching efficiency and SASP concentration appears in Fig. 3B. Quenching efficiency reached 84.68% when 200 μmol/L SASP was used. There was a good linear relationship when SASP concentration was 0.1–80 μmol/L. The quenching efficiency equation is: (F0-F)/F0 = 0.0079C-0.0017, R2 = 0.9981. The limit of detection for SASP (signal-to-noise ratio = 3) was 40 nmol/L. Compared to the reported method (Table S1), detecting SASP by our method has the advantages of a wider linear range, a lower detection limit [2,4], and a friendly environment [3], however, the detection limit of this method is higher than previously reported result [1].

The SASP fluorescence quenching mechanism on N-CQDs was investigated using UV–vis spectra (Fig. 4 A). Curve a and curve b are 1 μg/mL N-CQDs and 30 μmol/L SASP, respectively. Curve c is the spectrum of 1 μg/mL N-CQDs and 30 μmol/L SASP. Curve d is the theoretical spectrum which is derived by adding curve a to curve b. The experimental spectrum of curve c was very similar to the theoretical spectrum of curve d. There was no obvious deviation between peaks. This indicates that no complex formed between the synthesized N-CQDs and SASP. SASP has an absorption peak between 200 and 480 nm. The N-CQDs excitation spectrum was between 270–400 nm (Fig. 2B). The maximum excitation peak wavelength was 350 nm. The CQDs emission band was between 360 and 580 nm. The N-CQDs excitation spectrum and partial emission spectrums overlapped with the SASP absorption spectrum. SASP absorbed N-CQDs excitation, and partial emission lights which resulted in N-CQDs fluorescence intensity decreases. The results show that the quenching of N-CQDs is due to the internal filtration effect mechanism of SASP. The SASP concentration and F0/F relationship was linear (Fig. 4B). Fluorescence quenching fitted to the Stern–Volmer equation, F0/F = Ksv [Q] + 1, where F0, and F, are the fluorescence intensities showing the absence, or presence, of SASP, respectively. KSV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant and [Q] is the SASP concentration. Fluorescence lifetimes were also characterized in order to better understand the SASP/N-CQDs quenching mechanism. Fluorescence lifetimes with, and without, SASP were also investigated (Fig. 4C). Using the method described in Section 2.4, when there was no SASP, the N-CQDs average fluorescence lifetime was 5.36 ns. When SASP was added, the average fluorescence lifetime was 5.34 ns. Fluorescence lifetime was virtually unchanged when SASP was added to the CQDs. This indicates a static quenching process. The N-CQDs quenching fluorescence mechanism is a combination of internal filtration effect and static quenching.

The effects of some medications, amino acids, proteins, and minerals in plasma on the N-CQDs fluorescence quenching were investigated in order to study N-CQDs selectivity and sensitivity to SASP detection. Under the same experimental conditions, sulfonamide, aspirin, amoxicillin, LVFX, and sinomenine fluorescence spectroscopies were detected. The other medications had little effect on N-CQDs fluorescence quenching except for SASP (Fig. 5 A). Plasma contains a number of amino acids, proteins, carbohydrates, and minerals. This research selected interference from some substrates of cysteine, glucose, urea, human serum albumin, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ga2+, Fe3+, Cl−, PO4 3-, and SO4 2- in order to study how some biological molecules and ions in plasma might interfere with SASP detection (Fig. 5B).

Fluorescence quenching by SASP was obvious, but almost no fluorescence quenching was found in other biomolecules or ions. This indicates that the presence of biomolecules and ions in plasma does not affect SASP detection. Combined with the results of this study, the developed fluorescence sensor detects SASP with high sensitivity and accuracy and can be used to detect SASP in mouse plasma.

The mouse plasma samples were treated using the methods described in Section 2.6. The SASP concentration was 6.73 μmol/L in mice plasma at 2 h post-administration of 5 mg SASP/kg weight. The recovery experiment was carried out. The SASP recovery range was between 96% and 101%. RSDs ranged from 2.6% to 3.1% (Table S2). The method is relatively precise and can be used to detect SASP in practical samples.

This paper reports the successful synthesis of water-soluble N-CQDs from Ginkgo leaves using a hydrothermal method. They were then used to determine SASP in mice plasma. The method has several advantages including wide linear range, low detection limit, simple operation, low cost, green environment, and appears to be harmless. It provides a new method for the determination of SASP in plasma.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 21765004 and 21365006) and by the Guangxi Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 2014GXNSFDA118004 and 1598025-4), and by the Innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (YCSZ2013039). The research fund of State Key Laboratory for Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources (Guangxi Normal University) (CMEMR2017-A5) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpba.2018.11.025.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
